destinations.evidence: Engine launch installs a blocking handler for the networked pipeline that calls the pool canonical-state side-effect hook on the backfilled tip.
Execution stage runs the blocking handler after block execution during staged backfill.
destinations.snippet: if let Some(blocking_handler) = &self.blocking_handler { let chain = Chain::new(...); blocking_handler(&chain); }

destinations.evidence: Port scheduler address helper parses SCHEDULER_ADDRESS and falls back to 0x56e776BAE2DD60664b69Bd5F865F1180ffB7D58B.
Nodebuilder add-ons use the same env override and fallback Scheduler address.

equivalence_argument: Mapped across port and nodebuilder: staged backfill has a blocking canonical-state handler, and Scheduler address selection is env-overridable with aligned fallback.
rationale: These candidates are not the core payload inclusion path but preserve scheduler state availability and address correctness needed by downstream scheduled payload behavior.
statement: Pipeline backfill must install a blocking handler that processes canonical state transactions for the backfilled chain tip, and scheduler initialization must use the secure-deployer default Scheduler address when SCHEDULER_ADDRESS is unset while still allowing the environment override.
suggested_falsification_tests: Run staged backfill over a block with canonical async/scheduled side effects and assert pool on_blocking_canonical_state_change is called.
Unset and set SCHEDULER_ADDRESS in port/nodebuilder address helpers and assert fallback/override alignment.
summary: Backfill blocking handler and scheduler address override/fallback are mapped.
test_or_falsification_candidate: Backfill execution stage should call blocking handler for each executed block tip; Scheduler address override tests should match across port and nodebuilder.
title: Backfill and scheduler configuration keep scheduler state available
